major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: The definition by German professor G. Martthess in his book "The Properties of Ground Water" is: "Groundwater contaminated by human activities is groundwater in which the total dissolved solids and total suspended solids content exceeds the maximum allowable concentration set by domestic or international drinking water and industrial water standards, either directly or indirectly due to human activities; naturally occurring groundwater, which is not influenced by human activities, may also contain components that exceed these standards, and in such cases, it can be defined as contaminated based on the phenomenon of certain components exceeding natural variability." He believes that groundwater is subject to contamination regardless of human activities or natural conditions. The standard for measuring groundwater contamination is based on the drinking water and industrial water standards established domestically or internationally. French professor J.J. Fried in his book "Groundwater Pollution" states: "Contamination is the alteration of the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of water, which typically limits or hinders the use of groundwater in various aspects." American scholars D.W. Miller and others mention in their paper: "The terms Contamination and Pollution are synonymous, meaning the extent to which the quality of natural water is altered due to human activities to the point of being unsuitable... The natural result of groundwater movement through aquifers can also lead to an increase in the concentration of one or more components, a phenomenon referred to as 'mineralization'." He believes that the deterioration of groundwater quality caused by human activities is pollution, while the increase in concentration of one or more components under natural conditions is termed "mineralization." R.A. Freeze and J.A. Cherry in their book "Groundwater" state: "Any dissolved substances entering the water environment due to human activities, regardless of whether their concentration reaches a level that significantly deteriorates water quality, are referred to as contaminants, while the term pollution is a specialized term for conditions where contaminant concentrations have reached levels unacceptable to people." They believe that the standard for measuring groundwater contamination is when the level of pollution has reached an unacceptable degree. Lin Fengnian and others in "Environmental Hydrogeology" state: "Any phenomenon in which groundwater quality changes towards deterioration under the influence of human activities is collectively referred to as groundwater pollution." Regardless of whether this phenomenon causes water quality deterioration to a degree that affects usability, as long as this phenomenon occurs, it is considered pollution. As for the phenomenon of certain components in groundwater becoming relatively enriched or depleted in a natural environment, leading to water quality deterioration, it should not be regarded as pollution but rather termed "natural anomalies." Therefore, determining whether groundwater is polluted must meet two conditions: first, the water quality is deteriorating; second, this change is caused by human activities. The "Water Pollution Prevention and Control Law of the People's Republic of China" defines water pollution as: "Water pollution refers to the phenomenon where the intervention of certain substances leads to changes in chemical, physical, biological, or radioactive characteristics, thereby affecting the effective use of water, harming human health, or damaging the ecological environment, resulting in water quality deterioration." The definition of groundwater pollution in the "China Environmental Impact Assessment" (training material) compiled by the National Environmental Protection Agency is: "Groundwater pollution occurs when the pollutants exceed the self-purification capacity of groundwater, resulting in changes to its composition and properties." The concept of "pollution of groundwater from abandoned mines" in this article aligns with the provisions of the "Water Pollution Prevention and Control Law of the People's Republic of China," asserting that in closed mining areas, the high-hardness, acidic mine water from abandoned tunnels and old pits enters the target aquifer (the aquifer from which humans extract water for industrial and agricultural use) through various means, causing the water quality in that layer to deteriorate and affecting its usability.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text:
a. In terms of the classification of pollution sources, the pollutants entering groundwater can originate from human activities or natural processes. The pollution source referred to in this article belongs to anthropogenic industrial pollution sources, specifically the polluted water from coal mining strata. 

b. From the perspective of groundwater pollution methods, groundwater pollution can be classified into two types: direct pollution and indirect pollution. The pollution source referred to in this article is classified as direct pollution, which involves the discharge of pollutants underground. This means that whatever is present in the pollution source is also present in the groundwater, with the components of the groundwater directly originating from the pollution source and being consistent with it. The chemical properties of the pollutants do not change during their migration underground.

c. In terms of pollution pathways, there are various ways in which groundwater can be polluted, which can generally be categorized into four types: 
① Intermittent infiltration type. Atmospheric precipitation or other irrigation water carries pollutants through the unsaturated zone, periodically infiltrating into the aquifer, primarily polluting the unconfined groundwater. Pollution caused by leaching from solid waste piles falls into this category. 
② Continuous infiltration type. Pollutants continuously infiltrate into the aquifer with water, mainly also polluting unconfined groundwater. Areas where wastewater accumulates (such as wastewater channels, wastewater ponds, wastewater infiltration wells, etc.) and the continuous leakage from contaminated surface water bodies lead to groundwater pollution, which also belongs to this category. 
③ Overland flow type. Pollutants are transferred from already contaminated aquifers (or naturally saline layers) to uncontaminated aquifers (or naturally fresh layers) through overland flow. This includes interlayer overland flow caused by groundwater extraction, overland flow through hydrogeological windows, and overland flow through well casings. Pollutants are transferred into other aquifers through interlayer overland flow, which occurs via hydrogeological "windows," or through human-made pathways (such as improperly designed well casings, damaged old well casings, etc.), or due to changes in groundwater hydrodynamic conditions caused by human mining activities that alter the direction of overland flow, allowing pollutants to migrate over large areas through weak aquitards into other aquifers, with the pollution source being old well water or waste mine water. Diagrams of groundwater overland flow pollution are shown in Figures 3-1 and 3-2. The pollution source referred to in this article belongs to the overland flow type. 
④ Runoff type. Pollutants enter the aquifer through underground runoff, contaminating unconfined or confined groundwater. Pollutants entering the aquifer through underground karst channels fall into this category.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text:
The application of early warning systems for groundwater pollution is just beginning. Researching the theoretical framework for groundwater pollution early warning and establishing a computer-based early warning system holds significant practical importance. Groundwater pollution early warning differs from traditional groundwater quality assessment and forecasting; it uses changes in groundwater quality status as identification indicators, considering whether groundwater quality is transitioning from one state to another, thus exhibiting dynamic characteristics. 

(1) A proposal for establishing a groundwater pollution early warning system has been put forward, and studies have been conducted on the concept of groundwater pollution early warning and the design of the early warning system framework, providing new methods for groundwater quality management and decision-making. 

(2) The methodology for evaluating the current status of groundwater quality and analyzing its temporal and spatial changes has been researched. Groundwater quality assessment employs widely used methods such as single-factor evaluation, comprehensive index evaluation (National Standard GB/T14848-93), fuzzy comprehensive evaluation, and artificial neural network evaluation. To understand the spatial variation of water quality across the study area, a regional water quality status evaluation method based on spatial interpolation has been studied. Random methods such as grey models, time series analysis, and Spearman rank correlation coefficient methods have been used to predict and analyze trends in water quality changes. 

(3) A groundwater pollution risk assessment system and model have been established, considering three main factors: the inherent vulnerability of the aquifer, pollution source load risk, and pollution hazard. The methods for groundwater pollution risk assessment have been studied to make the evaluation more aligned with actual conditions, facilitating practical application. 

(4) A "Groundwater Pollution Early Warning System" has been developed, utilizing advanced development platforms and technologies, including the .Net development platform and the rich GIS control technology in ArcGIS Engine, along with the independent development of relevant components, completing the development of the early warning model and software. 

(5) The system adheres to the principle of practicality from design to implementation. It is user-friendly, easy to manage, and allows for simple data updates, featuring an optimized system structure and a comprehensive database system, along with a friendly user interface. 

(6) The system employs object-oriented design and interactive design concepts, with clear functional logic. It offers rich content and powerful functionalities, enabling water quality assessment, prediction, evaluation of the inherent vulnerability of aquifers, pollution source load risk assessment, pollution risk assessment, and water quality pollution early warning. The results can be presented in various forms, including intuitive maps, tables, charts, and composite maps formed by their combinations, to convey the information needed by users. 

(7) The system has strong data analysis capabilities, implementing mathematical models such as fuzzy comprehensive evaluation, neural networks, grey models, and time series analysis. It also possesses robust spatial analysis functions, including buffer analysis, overlay analysis, spatial interpolation, and the ability to create contour and surface maps from spatial data. 

(8) The groundwater pollution early warning system has computerized the theoretical model of groundwater early warning, establishing software for computer systems, providing a new and convenient management platform for water resource management and research departments, capable of delivering alert information and other data at any time, enabling managers to make timely decisions and effectively prevent issues before they arise. 

(9) An instance study of groundwater pollution early warning in the Qiqihar area using the groundwater pollution early warning system has shown that the Quaternary aquifer in the region has been significantly polluted. Additionally, the application in Qiqihar demonstrates that the software is user-friendly, powerful, and stable, with considerable potential for promotion.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Groundwater is not only a valuable natural resource that provides drinking water for human survival and development, but it is also an active component of the natural environment. In today's pursuit of sustainable development, scientifically defining the concepts of groundwater and groundwater pollution is a prerequisite for researching groundwater pollution prevention and control, laying the foundation for further protection and effective development of groundwater resources. This chapter, based on a scientific definition of the concept of groundwater, comprehensively evaluates the resource value and environmental value of groundwater. Groundwater plays an indispensable role in human survival and development, industry, and agriculture, while also serving the functions of water source storage and water resource balance, making it a participant in the water cycle. Additionally, an overview of the status of groundwater resources in our country is provided, as understanding the overall situation of groundwater resources is crucial for in-depth analysis of groundwater pollution prevention and control issues, holding significant importance.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Regarding the concept of groundwater pollution, there is currently no clear definition both domestically and internationally. However, with the rapid development of human society and urban residents' lives, especially since entering the industrialized society, the extensive use of groundwater in agriculture and industry has exacerbated the deterioration of the groundwater environment. Clarifying the concept of groundwater pollution holds significant practical and theoretical importance. Some scholars in our country have also made detailed distinctions regarding the concept of groundwater pollution. In "Groundwater Pollution and Prevention," Wang Yanxin and others explain the concept of groundwater pollution as follows: groundwater pollution refers to the phenomenon primarily caused by human activities in daily life that leads to the deterioration of groundwater quality, collectively referred to as groundwater pollution. He also pointed out that regardless of the extent of this pollution phenomenon, once the groundwater quality deteriorates due to human activities, it should be classified as groundwater pollution. However, the phenomenon where the mineral composition of groundwater in a purely natural geological environment exceeds that of groundwater under normal conditions, leading to differences in groundwater quality components, should not be regarded as groundwater pollution but rather defined as geological anomalies. Wang Yanxin emphasized that to determine whether it constitutes groundwater pollution, two essential conditions must be met: first, the deterioration of groundwater quality, and second, that this deterioration is caused by human activities.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Wang Bingchen, in "Groundwater Quality Models," believes that groundwater pollution is part of the overall water body pollution. There is a close relationship between surface water, groundwater, and atmospheric precipitation, and issues should be discussed from a holistic perspective on water resource pollution. Based on this, he defines groundwater pollution as the phenomenon where certain pollutants, microorganisms, or thermal energy enter water bodies in various forms and through various pathways, leading to the deterioration of water quality. This affects its normal use in national economic development and people's lives, harms individual health, disrupts ecological balance, and damages the beautiful environment.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: In foreign literature, the term "pollution" is used in two ways. One is "pollution," which describes the condition of pollution when the concentration of pollutants exceeds acceptable levels, indicating that human activities have degraded the natural water quality to a point where its usability is compromised. The other is "contamination," which refers to the situation where the concentration of pollutants has increased, but the water quality has not yet deteriorated. Generally, foreign literature categorizes groundwater pollution into two concepts: pollution and contamination. The term pollution is a general term that includes contamination as a type of pollution, suggesting that water quality deteriorates due to the influence of chemicals, thermal energy, or bacteria to a degree that, while it may not frequently pose a threat to human health, adversely affects daily life, municipal utilities, agriculture, and industrial use. Contamination, on the other hand, refers to the situation where water quality deteriorates due to chemical substances or bacterial pollution, leading to poisoning or disease transmission among residents. These definitions categorize groundwater pollution into that caused by human activities and that caused by natural environments. Due to the differences in pollution processes and degrees, the deterioration of groundwater quality resulting from human activities can be defined as pollution, while the increase in the concentration of one or more components in groundwater under natural conditions can be defined as mineralization.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text:
The sources of groundwater pollution are very diverse, with the discharge of surface water, precipitation, industrial "three wastes," domestic sewage, and household waste being the main causes of groundwater resource contamination. In rural areas, the extensive use of pesticides and fertilizers has exacerbated the deterioration of the groundwater environment. In summary, the main aspects include the following: 

1. Industrial pollution of groundwater resources. While industrial production utilizes groundwater, the large amounts of waste gas, wastewater, and waste residue it discharges directly or indirectly lead to the deterioration of groundwater quality, characterized by large areas and significant impact. The harm to groundwater from industrial production is primarily manifested in three ways: 
   - First, industrial waste gas. Many factories emit large quantities of toxic gases during production, such as sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and various acidic waste gases from the acid-making industry; steel metallurgy and non-ferrous smelting enterprises release sulfur dioxide, hydrogen chloride, and metal compounds like lead and manganese; the petroleum industry emits hydrogen sulfide, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide. These waste gases not only pollute the air, affecting residents' lives and health, but also allow pollutants to seep into the groundwater through precipitation.
   - Second, industrial wastewater. Untreated industrial wastewater discharged into rivers or other surface water bodies first pollutes surface water, then contaminates groundwater. Common pollutants include chlorinated solvents such as trichloroethylene (TCE) and perchloroethylene (PCE), widely used in dry cleaning, degreasing, and textile processing, which have a broad impact on groundwater quality and pose significant health risks, including miscarriage and neurological disorders. Chlorinated solvent contamination has been detected in many countries worldwide; in the 1980s, the detection rate of trichloroethylene in 232 groundwater pumping stations in the Netherlands was 67%, and 11 volatile chlorinated compounds were found in water sources of 39 small towns in the United States.
   - Third, industrial waste residue generally presents a patchy distribution, is highly toxic, and is difficult to eliminate.

2. Agricultural production pollution of groundwater resources. The agricultural impact mainly includes three aspects: 
   - First, the use of fertilizers and pesticides, which seep into the shallow groundwater through irrigation water and precipitation; 
   - Second, the discharge of domestic pollutants, which directly contaminates shallow groundwater through cesspools, infiltration wells, and garbage dumps, increasing water hardness; 
   - Third, irrigation with agricultural wastewater, which can lead to groundwater pollution and reduced crop yields with prolonged use.

3. Mining activities pollution of groundwater resources. Mining alters burial conditions and surface water flow, disrupting groundwater balance. Abandoned mine shafts and drill holes in mining areas may become conduits for groundwater pollution, and acidic mine water can dissolve salts, increasing the total hardness of groundwater.

4. Domestic pollution of groundwater resources. Urban domestic pollution has become increasingly evident. Since the 1980s, the urbanization rate has risen from 14% to 46% in 2007, with the urban population increasing from 210 million to 560 million. Rapid urban development has damaged the geological environment, leading to groundwater degradation. As of the second half of 2005, over 400 cities nationwide faced water shortages, with a general decline in groundwater quality and an expansion of pollution. In 253 major groundwater extraction areas, 63 showed worsening pollution trends. Over-extraction has also triggered geological disasters such as ground subsidence, fissures, seawater intrusion, and surface collapse.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Groundwater pollution not only affects industry and agriculture but also poses a potential threat to human health. Humans constantly rely on groundwater for their daily lives; once groundwater is contaminated, it can have severe consequences for those who depend on it long-term. While developing and utilizing groundwater resources, failing to actively protect them will worsen the ecological environment that humans rely on for survival, leading to irreparable losses. The impact of groundwater pollution on human life and production activities mainly includes the following aspects: First, the effect of groundwater pollution on industrial production. Changes in groundwater hardness can affect industrial water usage; increased hardness is detrimental to industrial production, posing significant risks to industrial facilities, potentially leading to increased equipment wear, the production of substandard or waste products, and necessitating softening treatment, which raises production costs and is unfavorable for promoting clean production processes. Second, groundwater pollution has significant harmful effects on agricultural production. The extensive use of contaminated groundwater for irrigation can lead to changes in soil composition, structural damage, and severe compaction over time, ultimately affecting cultivation. Additionally, wastewater contains a large amount of harmful minerals, such as excessive chloride ions and sulfates, which can inhibit crop growth, resulting in reduced yields and posing health risks to humans. Finally, the impact of groundwater pollution on human health. After groundwater is contaminated, the increase in unsuitable minerals for drinking water poses a threat to human health.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Surface pollution has severely impacted groundwater contamination. The extensive use of pesticides and fertilizers in rural areas, along with the use of wastewater for irrigation, and pollution from gas stations, industrial sites, and municipal landfill sites, exacerbates the deterioration of the groundwater environment, leading to groundwater pollution. Nitrogen pollution in shallow groundwater caused by the use of pesticides, fertilizers, or wastewater irrigation is widely distributed in agricultural areas within plains and basins, as well as in vegetable-growing areas near cities. Notable regions include the North China Plain, the Yangtze River Delta, the Pearl River Delta, the Songliao Plain, and the Sanjiang Plain. The leakage of pollutants from gas stations, various industrial enterprises, and livestock farms has a particularly pronounced impact on groundwater quality. Gas stations across the country have a significant effect on the groundwater environment. Sampling results from the North China Plain indicate that 20% of gas stations have leaks or safety hazards, and shallow groundwater near these stations has been contaminated to varying degrees; the maximum concentration of total petroleum hydrocarbons in the soil can reach 2936.6 mg/kg. There have been oil leakage incidents at gas stations in Beijing, contaminating water sources and forcing the closure of water treatment plants. Industrial waste landfill and the discharge of toxic wastewater and residues, especially from chromium and arsenic waste sites lacking anti-seepage measures, severely pollute groundwater, causing widespread contamination with chromium and arsenic. According to a 2002 survey by the China Environmental Monitoring Center of 47 key environmental protection cities and 54 landfills that basically meet construction requirements, the groundwater exceedance rate in these landfills was 88.6%, with the main exceedance items being permanganate index, total coliform bacteria, and ammonia nitrogen, with some indicators exceeding standards by tens of thousands of times, raising serious concerns about groundwater pollution.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text:
Facility agriculture, due to its unique production environment and frequent cultivation practices, continuously impacts soil environmental quality, leading to the accumulation of heavy metals and synthetic organic compounds. The long-term accumulation of heavy metals and organic substances can result in soil-groundwater pollution, posing potential risks to agricultural ecosystems and human health. Therefore, conducting research on the characteristics, sources, and ecological risks of soil-groundwater pollution in facility agricultural areas is of significant practical value for the prevention and control of water and soil pollution, as well as for ecological safety and human health protection. 

This paper focuses on the facility agricultural areas of Jingyang County and Fuping County in Shaanxi Province as typical study regions, examining the soil-groundwater system. Through field surveys, sampling tests, multivariate statistics, and risk assessments, it systematically analyzes the concentration levels, pollution status, and spatial distribution characteristics of typical heavy metals and phthalate esters (PAEs). It also elucidates the sources and contributions of heavy metals and PAEs in the soil-groundwater system, revealing the potential risks of heavy metal and PAE pollution to agricultural ecosystems and human health in facility agricultural environments. The main findings of this study are as follows:

(1) The spatial distribution and pollution characteristics of heavy metals and PAEs in the soil-groundwater of facility agricultural areas were clarified. The relationship between the main microbial community structure in the soil and the years of crop cultivation and landform types was elucidated, providing foundational information for analyzing pollution sources in the study area. Compared to the background values of soil in Shaanxi Province, the contents of manganese (Mn), cadmium (Cd), and lead (Pb) in the soil exceeded normal levels, with Cd showing the most significant exceedance. The highest concentration of butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP) in the soil of the study area was 125.06 μg/kg, followed by di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) at 86.20 μg/kg, both of which did not exceed the limits. The concentration of mercury (Hg) in the groundwater of the study area generally exceeded the national "Groundwater Quality Standards" Class III, with a exceedance rate of 100%; Hg concentrations peaked in the alluvial plain and the central loess plateau area, while being lower in the peripheral regions. Four types of PAEs were detected in the groundwater, with the concentrations of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and DEHP significantly exceeding the "Sanitary Standards for Drinking Water." The predominant microorganisms in the soil of facility agricultural areas were Actinobacteriota and Proteobacteria, with the alpha diversity index showing an initial increase followed by a decrease with the increase in planting years; the abundance of chemoheterotrophic and aerobic chemoheterotrophic microorganisms was relatively high.

(2) The pollution levels of heavy metals and PAEs in the soil-groundwater were accurately assessed, identifying the main pollution factors in the water-soil environment, providing reference data for pollution control. Using single factor indices and the Nemerow comprehensive pollution index, it was found that the soil heavy metals were at a strong pollution level, with Cd being the main pollutant. PAEs were at a moderate pollution level, with BBP as the primary pollutant. Both heavy metals and PAEs in the groundwater were at a strong pollution level, with Hg, DBP, and DEHP being the main pollutants, and the pollution degree gradually decreasing along the groundwater flow.

(3) The sources of heavy metal and PAE pollution in the soil of facility agricultural areas were identified, and the main contributions of each pollution source were quantitatively evaluated, providing a scientific basis for prioritizing pollution source management in facility agricultural areas. Through principal component analysis (PCA) and positive matrix factorization (PMF), four pollution sources for heavy metals and PAEs in the soil were identified: plastic film pollution source, domestic pollution source, pesticide and fertilizer pollution source, and a mixed source of natural and agricultural sources. Among these, Cd and Pb mainly originated from the plastic film pollution source, accounting for 18.14%; DNOP, BBP, and DEHP mainly came from domestic pollution sources, accounting for 20.01%; Zn, Cu, and Cr mainly originated from pesticide and fertilizer pollution sources, accounting for 22.75%; Fe, Mn, Cr, Cu, Zn, Ni, BBP, and DEHP were from the mixed source of natural and agricultural sources, accounting for 39.10%.

(4) The ecological risks of heavy metals and PAEs in the soil-groundwater of facility agricultural areas were assessed, revealing the health risk levels of heavy metals and PAEs in different landform units for human health, providing targets for pollution risk prevention and control in the study area. Among the heavy metals in the surface soil of the study area, Cd posed the greatest ecological risk, followed by Pb. PAEs were in a non-risk state in both surface and profile soils. The potential ecological risks of heavy metals and PAEs in the groundwater were at severe levels, with Hg and DBP classified as high pollution risk factors. The total non-carcinogenic and carcinogenic risks of soil pollutants in the study area were greater for children than for adults, with the risk index for children exceeding the threshold. The main health risk factors were heavy metals Cd, Cr, and Pb. In the groundwater, the non-carcinogenic risk for adults was lower than that for children, but the carcinogenic risk was higher, exceeding the threshold, with heavy metal Cr being the main health risk factor.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: The refining industry, as one of the pillar industries of the national economy, has promoted rapid economic development over the years. However, issues of soil and groundwater pollution due to non-standard operations or unexpected events have frequently emerged. Groundwater pollution is often concealed and complex, making it urgent to identify and conduct targeted remediation. Investigations have revealed that in a certain industrial park in the northwest, leaks of heavy metals, inorganic salts, and organic substances due to equipment failures or improper operations have led to serious soil and groundwater contamination. Therefore, this study focuses on the industrial park in the northwest, conducting an investigation of the pollution situation. Based on measured water quality geological data, the study employs single factor index and Nemero index evaluation methods to assess the current water quality status. Health risk assessments are conducted using the evaluation model from the "Guidelines for Health Risk Assessment of Groundwater Pollution." The MODFLOW software is used to establish a groundwater solute transport model to simulate the current pollution status and predict pollution migration trends, as well as to study the effects of different extraction treatment plans, providing a theoretical basis for the design of groundwater pollution extraction treatment plans in the region. The study yields the following results: (1) The assessment of water quality status reveals that the groundwater pollution level in the study area is very serious, with manganese and volatile phenols as the main pollutants. The health risk assessment indicates that the non-carcinogenic risks of manganese and volatile phenols are at unacceptable levels, and the carcinogenic risks of arsenic, nickel, benzene, and toluene are also unacceptable, significantly impacting human health. (2) By establishing a reliable solute transport model, the study inverses the pollution source information for manganese and volatile phenols and simulates the current pollution plume situation, which is currently dispersed and still in the early diffusion stage. The maximum pollution concentration for manganese reaches 190.68 mg/L, and for volatile phenols, it reaches 53.12 mg/L, both exceeding the groundwater Class III water quality standard by thousands of times. (3) If the pollution source is cut off without remediation measures, during a 20-year migration process, the maximum concentration of the pollution plume continuously decreases, while the area continues to expand. Over time, there is a theoretical possibility of the pollution plume migrating to downstream residential areas, affecting residents' health. (4) For the manganese pollution from Company A, remediation plans were designed including only extraction wells, extraction wells combined with impermeable curtains, and extraction wells combined with impermeable curtains and injection wells. Simulation results indicate that installing impermeable curtains downstream of the pollution plume can effectively inhibit its spread downstream; however, the closer the extraction wells are placed to the downstream side of the pollution plume, the less favorable it is for pollution remediation, accelerating the plume's downstream spread. (5) For the volatile phenol pollution from Company B, remediation plans were designed including extraction wells combined with impermeable curtains and extraction wells combined with impermeable curtains and injection wells. Simulation results show that the overall pollution level significantly decreases after 10 years of operation for all plans; however, the presence of water-blocking faults makes it difficult to significantly reduce the area of the pollution plume. The placement of injection wells can, to some extent, promote the migration of pollutants out of the fault zone, improving remediation efficiency. The results of this study can provide references for exploring the level of groundwater pollution in the region, conducting reasonable groundwater pollution remediation, and preventing the continuous migration of pollutants downstream.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: The extraction-replenishment method is a traditional groundwater pollution remediation technology. To enhance site treatment capacity and efficiency while reducing water resource loss, treated groundwater is often replenished back into the aquifer, creating a cyclic flow field. This is known as the extraction-replenishment treatment system, which mainly includes two replenishment methods: injection wells and surface infiltration. A key aspect of designing an extraction-replenishment treatment system is predicting and delineating the groundwater circulation zone and calculating the recovery rate, which is the ratio of the cyclic flow to the extraction flow. Due to the influence of lateral groundwater flow in its natural state, achieving a recovery rate of 100% is generally difficult. Understanding how the cyclic flow field and recovery rate of the extraction-replenishment treatment system change systematically under the influence of lateral flow remains a challenging issue. This paper generalizes typical one-extraction-one-replenishment systems, one-extraction-two-replenishment systems, and single-well extraction-circular infiltration systems based on the assumptions of an infinite planar space, uniform lateral flow intensity, a horizontal impermeable bottom, and a homogeneous isotropic aquifer. Using hydrodynamic analysis and numerical simulation methods, it explores the formation of the circulation zone and the variation of recovery rates under the influence of lateral flow. The main findings and insights of this study are as follows: (1) To improve the accuracy of circulation zone delineation, a particle tracking algorithm based on flow functions was proposed to identify streamlines near well points in planar two-dimensional flow, referred to as the SFB method. Compared to the existing Pollock method and fourth-order Runge-Kutta method, the SFB method demonstrates higher accuracy. For finite difference numerical simulation methods, a correction formula for well point water levels in the unconfined aquifer flow model was proposed, which can be used to optimize model cell sizes and achieve more accurate streamlines by placing particles around wells. (2) Using the superposition method of potential functions and flow functions in a planar two-dimensional flow field for dimensionless analysis, theoretical formulas for delineating the circulation zones of one-extraction-one-replenishment and one-extraction-two-replenishment systems and calculating their recovery rates were derived. Coupled with groundwater flow numerical simulations (based on MODFLOW) and various particle tracking techniques, the study investigated the variation characteristics of water quality point residence times in the circulation zone. The results indicate that the morphology, quantity, and recovery rate of the circulation zone are primarily influenced by the direction of lateral groundwater flow, the ratio of single-width flow to extraction flow, and the geometric parameters of well layouts, exhibiting a nonlinear relationship. When lateral groundwater flow is present, placing the recharge well in the upstream area of the extraction well can increase the recovery rate, although the residence time correspondingly decreases. (3) By employing the Dupuit-Forcheimer assumption and the flat upper boundary assumption, the analytical solution for the flow field of a single-well extraction-circular infiltration system was obtained. The fourth-order Runge-Kutta method was used for particle tracking to identify three-dimensional streamlines, determining the variation characteristics of the infiltration water circulation zone, penetration depth, and recovery rate. The recovery rate is influenced not only by lateral flow but also varies with changes in the filter pipe length of the extraction well. (4) A comparative design study of the extraction-replenishment treatment system was conducted using a test site in Beijing as a case study, indicating that when the extraction flow rate is low, the recovery rate of the infiltration method is lower than that of the injection well, whereas when the extraction flow rate is high, the differences in recovery rates among different replenishment methods are minimal.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Under the national promotion of green mine construction and the background of "dual carbon" goals, the solid waste backfill management of the Fushun West Open-pit Mine not only reduces numerous geological disaster issues in the mining area but also enhances the resource utilization rate of solid waste materials around the mine. After solid waste is backfilled into the open pit, long-term soaking by groundwater and rainwater leaching may cause certain pollution to the water environment surrounding the mining area, posing potential safety hazards to the drinking water of nearby residents. Therefore, it is crucial to predict and control the groundwater pollution and risks resulting from solid waste backfill. Taking the Fushun West Open-pit Mine as the research object and focusing on specific backfill plans, laboratory experiments were conducted to optimize the backfill material ratio and explore the release patterns of pollutants from the backfill materials. Based on field investigations and data collection of the hydrometeorology and geological information around the mining area, the groundwater seepage patterns after the closure of the open pit were studied. Predictions of groundwater pollution from solid waste backfill after the closure of the open pit were made using GMS, and health risk assessments of groundwater pollution from solid waste backfill were conducted using GMS in conjunction with GIS. The research findings are as follows: (1) Through BBD response surface experimental analysis, the optimal mix ratio for the solid waste cementitious filling body (SWCFB) material with the strongest impermeability is: solid mass concentration of 67.50%, alkaline slag content accounting for 11.46% of solid mass, steel slag accounting for 51.49% of solid mass, and shale slag accounting for 37.05% of solid mass. Static leaching test results indicate that the static leaching pollutant concentration of SWCFB material is lower than that of the raw materials, demonstrating a certain solidification effect on pollutants. Dynamic column test results show that the leaching of Fe and Cr in soil, mudstone, and sandstone exhibits a certain lag, with the adsorption capacity of the three media for Fe and Cr ranked as soil > mudstone > sandstone. (2) By establishing a three-dimensional mathematical model of groundwater seepage in the Fushun West Open-pit Mine, combined with water balance analysis and predictions of groundwater levels and flow after the closure of the mine, the results indicate that the main recharge method in the mining area is rainfall infiltration, with a recharge amount of 51,329 m³·d⁻¹, and the main drainage method is through various drainage pump stations, with a drainage amount reaching 73,343 m³·d⁻¹. After the closure of the mine and cessation of drainage, the groundwater flow field transitions from a precipitation funnel converging towards the center to a stable flow field moving northwest, ultimately resulting in a groundwater level in the pit reaching +72 m. (3) A multi-linear regression analysis (MLR) was used to establish a groundwater pollution solute transport model considering the combined effects of biological, chemical, and seepage factors. Using GMS, predictions were made regarding groundwater pollution resulting from solid waste backfill materials under different times, backfill heights, and backfill plans. The results indicate that Fe and Cr diffuse from the bottom of the pit to the surroundings as groundwater levels rise, gradually migrating northwest by 2060, with Fe reaching the south bank of the Hunhe River by 2080. The diffusion range and distance of the organic pollutant naphthalene (Nap) under biodegradation are not significant; the concentrations of Fe, Cr, and Nap in the pit increase with the height of backfill, and as the concentration of pollutants in the pit significantly increases, the impact of biodegradation on Nap diminishes. Compared to backfill plan one, the higher the backfill height of SWCFB material at the bottom of the pit, the stronger the impermeability, allowing for greater adsorption of Fe and Cr during the water storage phase in the pit. The microporous structure makes it difficult for the pollutants that have infiltrated the material to leach out again, ultimately reducing the concentration of pollutants. (4) Predictions of health risks caused by groundwater pollution from solid waste backfill after the closure of the open pit were made using GMS in conjunction with GIS. The results indicate that health risks due to Fe and Nap are low and negligible, while the health risk index for Cr, although below the acceptable risk limit set by the US EPA, reaches a medium risk level and should be taken seriously. The health risk for adult females exposed to pollutants is slightly higher than that for males, and children face the greatest health risk from Cr. The impermeability of SWCFB material can reduce health risks resulting from solid waste backfill; when the height of SWCFB material backfilled at the bottom of the pit is 20 m, it can significantly reduce the health risk for children affected by Cr. This paper contains 76 figures, 20 tables, and 104 references.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: The phenomenon of heavy metal pollution in China's soil is severe, and the remediation of heavy metal-contaminated soil is urgent. Solidification/stabilization (S/S) is widely applied in the remediation of actual heavy metal-contaminated sites due to its economic and efficient advantages. This technology reduces the leaching capacity of heavy metals in the soil; however, it does not decrease the total amount of heavy metals in the soil, which may still be re-released and migrate downwards into groundwater with rainfall, causing secondary pollution. A pressing issue is how to scientifically and objectively assess the groundwater pollution risk of solidified/stabilized heavy metal sites to ensure the ecological environment of the remediated sites. Based on this, this paper studies the long-term release risks of stabilized heavy metals in the environment and the effects of different influencing factors through soil column leaching and improved quantitative accelerated aging experiments. A groundwater pollution risk assessment model is established based on DRASTIC and the specific vulnerability of solidified/stabilized heavy metal sites, providing a new method for assessing the long-term risks of solidified/stabilized heavy metal soil and its pollution risk to groundwater. The main research findings are as follows: (1) The leaching experiment of stabilized As-contaminated soil columns shows that the leaching concentration of As is negatively correlated with the leachate pH and positively correlated with the leachate volume. The leaching experiment of stabilized Pb-contaminated soil columns indicates that stabilization can reduce the environmental risk of Pb-contaminated soil; simulating over 160 years, the environmental risk of soil with CaO as a stabilizer is lower than that with KH2PO4 as a stabilizer. The leaching concentration from the quantitative accelerated aging experiment is higher than that from the soil column leaching, suggesting that this assessment method may overestimate the actual site environmental risk, while the soil column experiment may underestimate it. (2) The environmental risk of stabilized heavy metal-contaminated soil is a dynamic and continuously increasing process; acid rain leaching causes the transformation of heavy metals from insoluble to soluble forms. Freeze-thaw cycles disrupt soil aggregates, accelerating soil acidification and further increasing the environmental risk of stabilized heavy metals. When the total amount of heavy metals is larger, even after stabilization, the long-term environmental risk may be greater than that of unstabilized soil. SEM-EDX results show that acid rain leaching causes the dissolution of Pb-containing precipitates, with residual forms continuously dissolving and transforming into other unstable forms or being directly released, leading to an increase in the environmental risk of stabilized heavy metal soil. (3) Combining experimental conclusions and literature research, based on DRASTIC and the specific vulnerability of solidified/stabilized heavy metal contaminated sites, 18 evaluation indicators are determined from four evaluation factors, establishing a groundwater pollution risk assessment system for solidified/stabilized heavy metal sites. The comprehensive weights of the four influencing factors—site hazard, pollutant stability, aquifer vulnerability, and natural conditions—are 0.1894, 0.3508, 0.3508, and 0.1092, respectively, and the risk levels are divided into five categories using an equal interval method. (4) An actual site was selected as the study area, and groundwater pollution risk was assessed using numerical simulation and a comprehensive index model. Through Hydrus software simulation, under the direct landfill scenario, as the unsaturated zone reaches maximum adsorption capacity, after 7.25 years, the As released from stabilized soil will completely penetrate the unsaturated zone into groundwater, with a concentration reaching 73.90 μg/L. Through GMS software simulation, after 100 years, As exceeds the 10 μg/L range and migrates downstream up to 135 m; by 109 years, As begins to pollute downstream water systems. Based on numerical simulation results, the groundwater pollution risk level is assessed as high risk. Using the comprehensive index risk assessment model, under direct landfill conditions, the comprehensive groundwater pollution risk indices for As and Cd are 4.55 and 4.58, respectively, both indicating medium risk; after additional protective measures, the indices for As and Cd are 3.98 and 4.02, respectively, with As's risk to groundwater reduced to low risk, while Cd remains medium risk. The original site's hydrogeological conditions are weak, making groundwater susceptible to pollution. The direct landfill of stabilized soil poses risks; to further reduce risks, the terrain slope was increased to enhance surface drainage capacity, lowering the comprehensive groundwater pollution risk indices for As and Cd to 3.94 and 3.90, both categorized as low risk.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: Groundwater resources have become one of the most important sources of water globally. Currently, due to the increasing discharge of wastewater from industrial and agricultural activities as well as human life, the environmental issues related to groundwater are becoming increasingly severe, making the resolution of these problems particularly urgent. In the southwestern region of a certain city, there exists a small chemical factory that discharges a large amount of untreated wastewater into the groundwater, leading to numerous ecological and environmental issues such as soil and groundwater pollution in the vicinity of the factory. Remediation of the groundwater pollution area and improvement of groundwater environmental quality is a key aspect of subsequent groundwater management in this region. This study focuses on the chemical factory and the surrounding polluted groundwater area. Through a series of tasks including data collection, field surveys, drilling, geophysical exploration, field tests (pumping and infiltration tests), water sample collection, and water quality testing and analysis, the extent and degree of groundwater pollution in the study area have been determined. Among the groundwater pollutants, carbon tetrachloride is identified as the main characteristic pollutant, primarily concentrated in the area of the former Wangshidian small chemical factory and its southwest direction. The analysis of groundwater pollution characteristics in the study area reveals that various organic pollutants are widely distributed, with the pollution range largely overlapping, primarily located in the vicinity of the small chemical factory and downstream of its groundwater. The target pollutant, carbon tetrachloride, has the highest exceedance factor, with an exceedance rate of 54.4%, making it the most severely polluted component in the groundwater system of the entire study area. In terms of water chemistry, the average concentration of Cl- is the highest, at 177.15 mg/L. The groundwater chemical type near the polluted site is mainly Cl-Na·Ca, with ion concentrations in the groundwater primarily influenced by water-rock interactions and, to some extent, by evaporation concentration and human activities. This paper utilizes the MODFLOW module in the groundwater numerical simulation software GMS to establish a hydrogeological conceptual model of the study area, defining model boundaries and aquifer characteristics. By inputting hydrogeological parameters obtained from preliminary field experiments and model source-sink items, a groundwater flow model is further developed. The model is repeatedly adjusted based on observed water level values until it aligns well with actual conditions. Using the groundwater flow model, the MT3DMS module is employed to simulate the transport of carbon tetrachloride solute, setting its source discharge concentration at 552 mg/L, with a daily wastewater leakage of 0.5 m³/d over a continuous period of 730 days. Analysis of the pollutant transport trend reveals that carbon tetrachloride moves from the northeast to the southwest, ultimately forming an elliptical pollution area, with the groundwater flow direction as the long axis of the ellipse, while the concentration of pollutants significantly decreases downstream. During the remediation phase, the concentration of carbon tetrachloride organic pollutants in the groundwater is addressed regionally. The first phase of remediation targets high-concentration pollution areas using an extraction-treatment method, while the second phase employs a permeable reactive barrier (PRB) technology. Coupled with the MT3DMS module, numerical simulations of the remediation process are conducted to observe the remediation effects. The numerical simulation results indicate that the extraction-treatment method can reduce the total amount of pollutants, limit their diffusion, and decrease the pollution range. Initially, the pollutant removal efficiency is high, but the effectiveness diminishes in later stages; after 500 days of pumping, the maximum pollutant concentration has dropped to 0.89 mg/L. Following the use of the PRB for the low-concentration pollution plume, the remediation effect is found to be favorable, with the range of the low-concentration pollution plume gradually decreasing. After 4000 days of operation, the PRB system's remediation effect on pollutants has reached the expected value (Class III water limit < 2 μg/L), further indicating that groundwater numerical simulation software can effectively reflect remediation outcomes.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: With the continuous development of the socio-economic landscape, new pollutants such as pharmaceuticals and artificial sweeteners are widely used in people's daily lives. Due to the "persistence" of these new pollutants in the environment, their behavior and residual levels in water bodies have garnered significant attention. The unique geological structures of karst areas, such as sinkholes and fissures, allow pollutants to rapidly transfer from the surface to groundwater, thereby affecting groundwater quality. This study focuses on the unique characteristics of groundwater in karst regions, selecting Guiyang, a karst city, as the research subject. Systematic sampling was conducted on groundwater and typical pollution sources in Guiyang, investigating the concentration levels and spatial distribution characteristics of dissolved trace elements and new pollutants in groundwater from typical pollution sources. Finally, a comparative analysis was conducted on the identification effectiveness of groundwater samples based on conventional indicators and new pollutant indicators. The main findings of the study are as follows: (1) The six trace elements in the study area did not exceed the national standard limits for Class III groundwater. Based on the overall concentration of target trace elements in the study area, they can be divided into three groups: high abundance element Sr (>10μg/L), medium abundance element Ni (1–10μg/L), and low abundance elements Cd, Co, Cu, and Li (<1μg/L). In samples from different typical areas, trace elements exhibited distinct spatial distribution characteristics, with the six elements showing a distribution pattern of city center > urban-rural fringe > agricultural area, indicating that urban activities are an important factor influencing trace elements in groundwater. (2) Research on the distribution characteristics of four new pollutants in landfills and sewage treatment plants showed that caffeine and saccharin were detected at 100% in all landfill sampling points, with the highest concentration of caffeine reaching 1881.49 ng/L, followed by saccharin (195.26 ng/L). The detection concentrations of carbamazepine and acesulfame were generally low, with total detection concentrations ranging from ND to 10.41 ng/L. The total detection concentration range of the four new pollutants in the inflow and outflow of the sewage treatment plant was 16.02 to 7533.08 ng/L, with caffeine and acesulfame being the main target substances. The three sewage treatment processes (A2O process, FBBR, and CAST process) showed different removal rates for target new pollutants, with the A2O process achieving over 90% removal efficiency for caffeine and acesulfame; the removal efficiency for saccharin varied little among different processes, ranging from 40.58% to 79.02%, with the S1 sewage treatment plant showing the poorest removal effect. (3) All five target new pollutants were detected in Guiyang's groundwater, with caffeine, saccharin, and procymidone having a detection rate of 100%, while carbamazepine had the lowest detection rate at 48%. The highest detection concentrations were as follows: caffeine (6274.52 ng/L) > saccharin (209.47 ng/L) > acesulfame (92.65 ng/L) > procymidone (3.14 ng/L) > carbamazepine (0.26 ng/L). In groundwater from different regions, the concentration levels of caffeine, carbamazepine, acesulfame, and saccharin were higher in the city center and urban-rural fringe areas than in agricultural areas, while the spatial distribution of procymidone was the opposite, indicating that urban activities significantly influence caffeine, carbamazepine, acesulfame, and saccharin, while agricultural activities are a major factor affecting procymidone. (4) A comprehensive index for urban and agricultural areas in the study area was established based on traditional indicators and new pollutant indicators (including urban comprehensive index Wc and agricultural comprehensive index Wa), which differentiated groundwater samples. It was found that the urban comprehensive index for urban groundwater samples was higher than that for agricultural area samples, while the agricultural comprehensive index showed the opposite trend. The analysis results of the comprehensive index indicated that the combination of traditional indicators and new pollutant indicators provided a more pronounced classification effect for groundwater samples compared to using either set of indicators alone, suggesting that integrating new pollutant indicators with traditional tracer indicators is a more effective method for identifying the impacts of different source activities on groundwater environmental quality.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text:
With the development of society, construction sites with relatively simple geological conditions are gradually becoming saturated. To explore methods for evaluating groundwater environmental pollution in complex construction conditions, it is necessary to conduct targeted assessments of potential sudden pollution events that may occur in complex sites, providing theoretical support for reasonable site planning and disaster prevention and reduction. This paper selects earthquakes as the research object among the sudden pollution triggers and attempts to explore the environmental impact of earthquakes on groundwater pollution sites based on previous studies regarding the changes in permeability of aquifers after seismic disturbances, as well as the amplitude of permeability changes in porous and fractured aquifers post-earthquake. This aims to provide a reference for groundwater environmental evaluation in seismically active regions.

Taking the groundwater environmental impact assessment of a proposed high pollution risk facility in Yunnan as an example, a hydrological and geological survey was conducted over an area of 13.36 km² to analyze the geological and hydrogeological conditions of the site, obtaining necessary hydrogeological parameters through field tests. By combining the actual working conditions of the site, a reasonable generalization of the study area was performed, and a groundwater flow and solute transport model was established using Visual MODFLOW. Traditional scenarios of leakage due to building aging or uneven settlement were compared with non-traditional scenarios of earthquake-induced leakage through simulation.

Using the facility's maximum operational cycle as the simulation time boundary, the results indicate that under conditions of effective groundwater quality monitoring and timely investigation and repair of leakage sources, the pollution plume's extent post-earthquake, when pollutant concentrations peak, is nearly indistinguishable from that of leakage scenarios without an earthquake. The impact of earthquakes on groundwater pollution is primarily reflected in changes in pollutant concentrations and the long-term migration range of pollutants. In the absence of an earthquake, the peak concentration at the center of the pollution plume is 6.18% to 6.2% of the pollutant source concentration, which increases to 6.25% to 7% post-earthquake, with a rise of 0.8‰ to 8.2‰. The natural attenuation of the pollution plume shows a particularly significant effect from the earthquake; in acute leakage scenarios post-earthquake, the range of residual pollutants exceeding standards can reach up to 177.76% of that in slow leakage scenarios, while lead transitions from natural attenuation under slow leakage conditions to still having a small amount of excess residual.

For this study site, it is crucial to focus on the natural attenuation of COD, ammonia nitrogen, BOD, fluoride, and lead during the decontamination work after site closure. The study reveals that while the changes in aquifer permeability under seismic influence are recoverable, there exists a certain correlation between earthquakes and leakage. When the effects of both overlap, there are indeed abnormal changes in groundwater environmental pollution, characterized by increased concentration and prolonged duration. Therefore, when assessing and predicting groundwater environmental pollution risks in high-risk seismic areas, the continued impact of earthquakes on site pollution should be taken into account.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: As a major grain-producing country, China has a vast and widely distributed area of arable land, much of which is located in the alluvial fan areas at the foot of mountains in the northern part of the country. Due to the limited absorption of fertilizers by crops, excessive fertilization leads to a significant accumulation of agricultural fertilizers on the surface. With the influence of rainfall and irrigation, these fertilizers can enter the groundwater, causing pollution. In the northern alluvial fan areas of Henan Province, there are numerous farmlands and intense human activities, making it easy for the increased use of nitrogen fertilizers to result in excessive nitrate ion levels in the groundwater, affecting the safety of drinking water and agricultural water for local residents. Currently, there is limited research on the migration patterns of groundwater pollutants and risk assessments in the northern alluvial fan areas of Henan Province. Therefore, studying the migration patterns of groundwater pollutants and their risk assessments in the northern alluvial fan areas of Henan is crucial. This paper focuses on the northern alluvial fan of Henan Province, constructing a groundwater solute transport model based on non-point source pollution using relevant geological and hydrogeological data of the study area, with nitrate ions as the main pollutant for migration pattern analysis. A groundwater pollution risk assessment index system based on DRASTIC-LQ was established, incorporating groundwater depth (D), net recharge (R), aquifer type (A), soil type (S), terrain slope (T), vadose zone type (I), permeability coefficient (C), land use type (L), and groundwater quality (Q). The random forest algorithm was used to correct this system and evaluate and predict the pollution risk of shallow groundwater in the study area. The main conclusions are as follows: (1) Through descriptive statistics of the groundwater chemical characteristics in the study area, it was found that the shallow groundwater environment is weakly alkaline, with moderate spatial variability in TDS, total hardness, K+, Ca2+, NO3-, F-, total bacterial count, and oxygen consumption. The spatial variability of Na+, Mg2+, SO42-, and Cl- is significant and highly uneven. The chemical characteristics of groundwater in the study area mainly arise from the weathering and dissolution of carbonates, sulfates, silicates, and fluorine-containing minerals, with NO3- primarily sourced from the use of pesticides and fertilizers. An evaluation of shallow groundwater quality in the study area using the analytic hierarchy process, entropy weight method, and gray relational analysis revealed that 76.91% of the groundwater quality in the area is classified as Class III or above, while 23.09% is classified as Class IV, indicating overall good water quality. (2) A three-dimensional unsteady flow model for shallow groundwater in the northern alluvial fan of Henan was established using MODFLOW, with nitrate ions as the main pollutant. The pollution source strength was set in a non-point source recharge form, and the migration patterns of pollutants were analyzed for the next 1, 5, 10, and 20 years. The results indicated that the direction of pollutant migration primarily flows from northwest to southeast, with pollutants in the concentrated agricultural areas of the southwestern part of the study area also being influenced by groundwater recharge funnels, migrating and diffusing towards the northeast. The extent of the pollution plume in the study area continues to expand. After maintaining the current fertilization levels and continuously inputting nitrate nitrogen for 20 years, the area of the heavily polluted plume reaches 21.01 km², while the area of the moderately polluted plume increases to 9.9 times its initial size, with the plume gradually spreading downstream and approaching the Dasha River and Dashilao River, impacting the safety of residents' drinking water. (3) A groundwater pollution risk assessment model for shallow groundwater in the study area was constructed based on the random forest algorithm and DRASTIC-LQ, where the random forest algorithm was used to calculate indicator weights, reducing the subjectivity of determining weights solely using the DRASTIC model, making the weight calculation more reasonable. The inclusion of land use type and groundwater quality as two special vulnerability indicators in the index system provides a more comprehensive reflection of the pollution risk in the study area. A comparison of the correlation between nitrate ion concentration and risk revealed that the groundwater pollution risk assessment model based on the random forest algorithm is more suitable for evaluating the pollution risk of shallow groundwater in the northern alluvial fan of Henan. (4) Based on the current groundwater pollution risk status in the study area, it was found that 60.49% of the area is at moderate or above risk levels, while 39.51% is at low or below risk levels, indicating a significant risk of groundwater pollution. The distribution of high and above risk levels is roughly the same, primarily located in the northeastern and southwestern regions of the study area, while lower risk levels are found in the eastern and southern parts. Predictions of groundwater pollution risk for the next 1 and 5 years based on the groundwater flow model indicate that as the depth decreases, the risk of groundwater pollution increases. Therefore, in the northern part of the study area, it is advisable to appropriately increase groundwater extraction to reduce pollution risk, while in the southern part, where there is a certain range of recharge funnels, groundwater extraction should be reasonably controlled to maintain the pollution risk level.

major categories: Soil and water pollution and geochemical abnormal disasters
text: As our country's economy develops rapidly, a significant amount of soil and groundwater pollution issues have emerged, with pollutant leakage posing a serious threat to the safety of soil and groundwater resources. The methods for simulating pollutant migration mainly include analytical and numerical methods. Numerical simulations of pollutant migration involve steps such as establishing geometric models and setting initial and boundary conditions. Creating numerical models for numerous contaminated sites requires substantial time costs, and the results cannot be directly applied to site health risk assessments. Therefore, analytical solutions for pollutant migration are widely used for the early assessment of whether contaminated sites pose a threat to soil and groundwater. This paper establishes four potential composite models for environmental impact analysis of groundwater pollution sites and compares them with calibrated numerical model results, ultimately proposing a relatively optimal composite model for pollutant migration analysis. The main work and research findings are as follows: (1) In response to the migration patterns of pollutants in soil and groundwater, a coupling of one-dimensional and three-dimensional analytical models for pollutant migration was established, creating four composite models for environmental impact analysis of contaminated sites. These composite models can simulate the migration processes of pollutants in soil and groundwater under common geological conditions. (2) A three-dimensional finite element numerical model was established for cases with pollution sources on the aquifer surface, and its computational results closely matched the three-dimensional analytical solutions. Based on this, considering the location of pollution sources above the aquifer, a numerical model for pollutant migration was developed for a contaminated site in Denmark, showing that the distance from the pollution source to the top of the aquifer is a key factor in the environmental impact analysis of groundwater pollution sites. (3) By comparing the predicted groundwater pollutant concentration distributions from the four potential composite models with the numerical model results, it was found that only one composite model (Model 4 of this paper) had predictions that closely matched the numerical model results, allowing for rapid predictive analysis of pollutant migration using this composite model. (4) The relatively optimal composite model was applied to simulate and analyze a chemical pollution site in Tianjin. When petroleum substances leaked under abnormal conditions for 100 years, using a pollutant impact concentration of 0.04 mg/L as a baseline, the maximum horizontal migration distance in the aquifer was approximately 86 meters, leading to severe pollution of the underground aquifer. The predicted spatiotemporal distribution of pollutant concentrations provides a scientific basis for subsequent health risk assessments of the site.
